title: Computer Is Slow
cardText: Diagnose a computer that feels slow, delayed, or unresponsive.
Check whether the slowdown is caused by one app, low storage, high CPU, high memory, disk activity, updates, malware, or ageing hardware.
Separate normal background activity from faults that require cleanup, repair, or hardware upgrades.
text: This guide is for computers that turn on and display normally but feel slow to use.
Start by identifying whether the whole computer is slow or only one application.
Check common causes such as storage space, startup load, background updates, CPU usage, memory pressure, disk health, and malware.
Escalate when the system remains slow after software cleanup and basic checks.

identifyScope.title: Identify what is slow
body: Determine whether the whole computer is slow or only one program is affected.
A single slow app usually points to that app, its data, its network connection, or its settings.
A whole-system slowdown points more toward CPU, memory, disk, startup, updates, malware, or hardware limits.

checkSingleApp.title: Check the affected application
body: Close and reopen the affected application.
Check whether other apps run normally.
If only one program is slow, update it, clear its cache if appropriate, or reinstall it.

checkAppNetworkDependency.title: Check whether the app depends on internet or a server
body: Some applications feel slow because they are waiting on internet, cloud storage, email servers, databases, or shared network folders.
Test a local app such as Calculator, Notepad, or File Explorer.
If local apps are fast but cloud or network apps are slow, troubleshoot the network or service instead.

networkRelatedAppIssue.title: Application or network service issue likely
body: The slowdown appears limited to an app that depends on internet, cloud storage, email, or a server.
Check the service status, network connection, VPN, shared folder access, or application-specific settings.
The computer itself may not be the main cause.

recentOrLongTerm.title: Check whether the slowdown is recent
body: Ask whether the computer has always been slow or became slow recently.
A recent slowdown often points to updates, new software, malware, failed hardware, or low disk space.
A long-term slowdown often points to ageing hardware, too little RAM, mechanical hard drive performance, or too many startup programs.

checkRecentChanges.title: Check recent changes
body: Check whether new software, drivers, updates, browser extensions, antivirus tools, or startup apps were added recently.
Recent changes are useful because they create a likely before-and-after point.
Undo or disable suspicious recent changes one at a time and retest.

undoRecentChange.title: Undo or disable the recent change
body: Disable, uninstall, or roll back the recent change if safe to do so.
Restart the computer after making the change.
Then test whether normal performance has returned.

checkHardwareAge.title: Check whether the computer is underpowered
body: Older computers with low RAM, weak processors, or mechanical hard drives may be slow even when nothing is technically broken.
Check the system age, RAM amount, processor generation, and whether it uses an HDD or SSD.
A computer with a mechanical hard drive and low memory will often feel slow on modern Windows.

hardwareUpgradeLikely.title: Hardware upgrade likely needed
body: The computer appears to be limited by older or low-end hardware rather than a single fault.
The most effective upgrades are usually replacing an HDD with an SSD and increasing RAM.
If the computer is very old, replacement may be more sensible than repair.

checkDiskSpace.title: Check free disk space
body: Check how much free space is left on the system drive.
Windows can become slow when the main drive is nearly full because updates, temporary files, cache, and virtual memory need working space.
As a rough rule, try to keep at least 15–20 percent of the system drive free.

freeDiskSpace.title: Free disk space
body: Empty the recycle bin, remove temporary files, uninstall unused programs, and move large personal files to another drive if appropriate.
Use Windows Storage settings or Disk Cleanup for safe cleanup.
Avoid deleting files from system folders unless you know exactly what they are.

openTaskManager.title: Open Task Manager
body: Open Task Manager and check CPU, Memory, Disk, and Network usage.
Sort by each column to see what is using the most resources.
This helps identify whether the slowdown is caused by processor load, memory pressure, disk activity, or background network usage.

identifyHighUsage.title: Identify which resource is high
body: Check which Task Manager column is consistently high.
High CPU, high memory, high disk usage, and high network usage point to different causes.
Do not rely on a one-second spike. Look for repeated or sustained high usage.

cpuOrMemoryHigh.title: Check CPU and memory pressure
body: If CPU is high, identify the process using it.
If memory is high, check whether many apps, browser tabs, or background tools are open.
High memory use can make Windows rely on disk paging, which causes major slowdowns.

handleKnownHeavyProcess.title: Handle the heavy process
body: If the process is a normal app, close it or reduce what it is doing.
If it is a browser, close unused tabs and disable unnecessary extensions.
If it is antivirus, backup, indexing, or updates, allow it to finish or reschedule it.

scanForMalware.title: Scan for malware or unwanted software
body: Run a trusted antivirus or Windows Security scan.
Look for unknown startup entries, suspicious browser extensions, fake cleaners, toolbars, or pop-ups.
Malware and unwanted software often cause high CPU, high memory, browser redirects, or constant background activity.

removeThreats.title: Remove malware or unwanted software
body: Remove or quarantine the detected threats.
Restart the computer and run another scan.
If the system remains unstable or suspicious activity returns, escalate for deeper cleanup or backup-and-reinstall.

diskOrNetworkHigh.title: Check disk or network activity
body: If Disk usage is high, check whether Windows Update, antivirus scanning, cloud sync, or indexing is running.
If Network usage is high, check for cloud backup, large downloads, updates, or streaming.
Background activity can make a computer feel slow even when nothing is broken.

waitForBackgroundTask.title: Let background tasks finish
body: Allow updates, indexing, antivirus scans, or cloud sync to complete.
Restart the computer after the task finishes.
If the slowdown only happens during these tasks, reschedule them or reduce startup/background load.

checkDiskHealth.title: Check disk health
body: Check whether the drive is an HDD or SSD and look for disk health warnings.
Signs of drive problems include clicking noises, very slow file access, freezes, failed updates, missing files, or SMART warnings.
A failing drive can make the whole computer slow and should be treated as a data-risk issue.

backupAndReplaceDrive.title: Back up data and replace drive
body: A failing drive can cause slow performance and data loss.
Back up important files immediately.
Replace the drive and reinstall or clone the system if appropriate.

checkStartupPrograms.title: Check startup programs
body: Open Task Manager startup settings and review programs that start with Windows.
Disable unnecessary startup apps such as launchers, updaters, chat apps, helper tools, and vendor utilities.
Do not disable security software or drivers unless you know what they do.

disableStartupApps.title: Disable unnecessary startup apps
body: Disable non-essential startup programs.
Restart the computer and test performance again.
Reducing startup load can improve boot time and general responsiveness.

checkWindowsUpdates.title: Check Windows Update status
body: Open Windows Update and check whether updates are downloading, installing, failing, or waiting for restart.
Windows can feel slow during large updates or after updates while background tasks finish.
A repeatedly failing update can also cause repeated background load.

completeUpdates.title: Complete or repair updates
body: Allow updates to finish and restart when prompted.
If updates are stuck or repeatedly failing, use the Windows Update Failed guide.
Retest performance after updates complete.

checkBrowserLoad.title: Check browser tabs and extensions
body: Browsers can consume large amounts of memory and CPU, especially with many tabs or extensions.
Close unused tabs, disable unnecessary extensions, and test with a different browser if needed.
If only web browsing is slow, the issue may be browser, DNS, internet speed, or website related.

browserCleanup.title: Browser cleanup resolved issue
body: The slowdown was caused by browser load, extensions, or web content.
Keep unnecessary extensions disabled and reduce excessive open tabs.
If browsing remains slow, troubleshoot internet or DNS separately.

checkThermalThrottling.title: Check for overheating or throttling
body: Listen for loud fans and check whether the computer gets very hot.
Dust buildup, blocked vents, failed fans, or dried thermal paste can cause the CPU or GPU to slow itself down to prevent damage.
Laptops are especially vulnerable when used on beds, carpet, or dusty surfaces.

cleanCoolingSystem.title: Clean cooling and improve airflow
body: Power off the computer before cleaning.
Remove dust from vents and fans if safe to do so.
Ensure laptops are used on a hard surface and desktops have clear airflow around the case.

checkUserProfile.title: Test another user profile
body: Sign in with another user account if available.
A damaged or overloaded user profile can cause slow logins, desktop delays, broken search, and slow Explorer behaviour.
If another profile is fast, the issue may be profile-specific rather than system-wide.

profileIssueLikely.title: User profile issue likely
body: The computer performs better under another user profile.
Back up the affected user's files and consider creating a fresh profile.
Check login items, profile corruption, mapped drives, cloud sync, and desktop/file clutter.

finalPerformanceAssessment.title: Further performance diagnostics required
body: Basic checks did not identify a simple cause.
Next steps may include checking event logs, drive benchmarks, RAM testing, clean boot testing, reinstalling Windows, or assessing hardware upgrades.
If the computer has an HDD, low RAM, or very old hardware, upgrading or replacing it may be the most practical solution.
